title: Vintage Pew Bench
summary: A church-pew-style bench with a slide-out drawer hidden in the seat and a decorative mirrored-end design — built as two matching end assemblies joined into one bench.
description:
Both ends of this bench are built identically but assembled as mirror images of each other — the same principle behind any piece with two matching sides, and the point in the build where it's easiest to introduce an error is skipping ahead without checking that end two really is a mirror of end one, not a duplicate of it. Get that right before joining the ends together and everything downstream lines up.

The drawer rides on simple wood slides rather than metal hardware — no purchased slides, just a snug test-fit as you build. That keeps cost and complexity down, at the tradeoff of a slightly less smooth glide than metal slides would give; reasonable for a bench drawer that isn't opened often.

tools.note: Cuts the decorative curved end profile.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut two end panels at 32-1/4" and one seat panel at 36" from the Pine 1x12. Cut two end decorative caps at 11-1/4" from the Pine 1x4. Cut two end decorative trim at 11-1/4", one front apron at 36", two drawer sides at 11-1/2" and two drawer front and back at 34-1/4" from the Pine 1x6. Cut four legs at 36", two leg braces at 5-1/4" and two back bottom and front support at 36" from the Pine 2x2. Cut two front decorative trim at 16-1/4" and one seat back trim at 36" from the Pine 1x3. Cut one back top rail at 35", two drawer slides (wood) at 12" and one seat trim, front at 36" from the Pine 1x2. Cut two back supports at 36" from the Pine 2x4. Cut one back panel at 39" from the 1/4" plywood. Cut one drawer bottom at 11-1/2" from the 1/4" plywood. Cut one drawer face at 35-3/4" from the Pine 1x8. Allowing about 1/8" for each saw cut, that comes to one 10 ft Pine 1x12, one 2 ft Pine 1x4, two 8 ft Pine 1x6, three 8 ft Pine 2x2, two 4 ft Pine 1x3, one 8 ft Pine 1x2, one 8 ft Pine 2x4 and one 3 ft Pine 1x8. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw — several parts here are within half an inch of each other and are easy to mix up later.

steps.title: Build the two ends
steps.body: Build the two end assemblies. Drill 3/4" pocket holes on both sides of each end panel so the legs can be attached in the next step.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Attach the 2x2 legs to each end panel with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws through the holes drilled in the previous step. Build two leg sets, remembering each is a mirror of the other, not a duplicate.

steps.title: Add the decorative front piece
steps.body: Add the decorative front piece to each end, keeping the two ends built as mirror images of each other.

steps.title: Add the top
steps.body: Attach the decorative cap to the top of each end — remember the two ends are still mirrors of each other.

steps.title: Join the two ends
steps.body: Join the two end assemblies together to start forming the bench, attaching the back supports from the back side with pocket holes.

steps.title: Attach the front apron
steps.body: Attach the front apron between the two ends.

steps.title: Add the drawer slides
steps.body: Attach the wood drawer slides inside the bench.

steps.title: Build the drawer box
steps.body: Build the drawer to fit your actual opening, testing the fit as you go and leaving a little clearance for it to slide.

steps.title: Attach the drawer face
steps.body: Attach the drawer face to finish the drawer off, leaving an even gap around it on all sides. Add the drawer pull.

steps.title: Build and attach the seat
steps.body: Build the seat as its own assembly with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, then attach it to the bench.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body:
Attach the back panel with glue and brad nails. Quarter round can be added along the inside back edge as a finishing touch.

Sand every surface and finish.

images.alt: Vintage church-pew-style storage bench with drawer
